elements.sent-2411-001: In 1986, Albert proposed a Marsden-Weinstein reduction process for cosymplectic structures.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we present the limitations of this theory in the application of the reduction of symmetric time-dependent Hamiltonian systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: As a consequence, we conclude that cosymplectic geometry is not appropriate for this reduction.
elements.sent-2411-004: Motived for this fact, we replace cosymplectic structures by more general structures: mechanical presymplectic structures.
elements.sent-2411-005: Then, we develop Marsden-Weinstein reduction for this kind of structures and we apply this theory to interesting examples of time-dependent Hamiltonian systems for which Albert's reduction method doesn't work.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Marsden-Weinstein reduction of a Hamiltonian system on a symplectic manifold
elements.sent-2411-006: Symplectic manifolds play a key role in the Hamiltonian formulation of Classical Mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-007: As it is well known, given a configuration space $Q$ of a classical mechanical system, its cotangent bundle $T^*Q$, equipped with a canonical symplectic structure, is the fundamental element to describe Hamiltonian Mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-008: More precisely, for a Hamiltonian function $H:T^*Q\to {\Bbb R}$, Hamilton equations are just the integral curves of the corresponding Hamiltonian vector field (see, for instance, \cite{AM,MP, LM ).}
elements.sent-2411-009: In this context, a method that simplifies the integration of the dynamical equations of a symmetric Hamiltonian system and that sometimes provides new examples of symplectic manifolds, is the reduction process.
elements.sent-2411-010: A particular case is the so-called Marsden-Weinstein reduction \cite{MW (see also \cite{AM ).}}
elements.sent-2411-011: A Hamiltonian action of a Lie group $G$ on a symplectic manifold $(M,\Omega )$ is an action by symplectomorphisms such that there is a map $J\colon M\to \mathfrak{g ^*$, $\mathfrak{g ^*$ being the dual of the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ of $G$, satisfying \begin{equation}i_{\xi_M}\Omega=dJ_\xi,\qquad for all \xi \in {\mathfrak g},\end{equation} where $\xi_M\in \mathfrak{X (M)$ is the fundamental vector field corresponding to the Lie algebra element $\xi$ and $J_\xi\colon M\to \R$ is the real $C^\infty$-function on $M$ given by \begin{equation}J_\xi (x)=\langle J(x),\xi \rangle, for x \in M.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Moreover, $J$ is $G$-equivariant if it is equivariant with respect to the symplectic action $\phi$ and the coadjoint representation $Ad^*:G\times {\mathfrak g ^*\to {\mathfrak g ^*$.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Under this hypothesis (a Hamiltonian action with $G$-equivariant momentum map), given a regular value $\mu$ of $J$, if the isotropy subgroup $G_\mu$ for the coadjoint representation acts freely and properly on $J^{-1 (\mu )$ then $M_\mu=J^{-1 (\mu )/G_\mu$ is a smooth manifold and the Marsden-Weinstein reduction process shows that there is a naturally induced ``reduced" symplectic structure $\Omega_\mu$ on $M_\mu$.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: If, in addition, we have a $G$-invariant Hamiltonian function $H\colon M\to \R$ then, for each $\xi \in \mathfrak{g}$, the smooth function $J_\xi$ is a first integral of the Hamiltonian vector field $X_H$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Moreover, the Marsden-Weinstein reduction process allows to describe the reduced dynamics as the Hamiltonian dynamics with respect to a reduced Hamiltonian function $H_\mu\colon M_\mu\to \R$.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Cosymplectic and symplectic reduction for time-dependent Hamiltonian systems
elements.sent-2411-016: Cosymplectic structures provide an odd-dimensional counterpart to symplectic structures.
elements.sent-2411-017: A cosymplectic manifold $(M,\omega, \eta )$ is a $(2n+1)$-dimensional manifold $M$ equipped with a closed $2$-form $\omega\in\Omega^2(M)$ and a closed $1$-form $\eta\in \Omega^1(M)$ such that $\eta\wedge \omega^n$ is a volume form on $M$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Cosymplectic structures were first introduced by Libermann \cite{Lib and they appear, for instance, as the induced structure on the critical set of a $b$-symplectic manifold \cite{GMP .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In addition, they have been used in several geometric formulations of time-dependent Hamiltonian systems (see, for instance, \cite{ChLeMa ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: Given a cosymplectic structure on a manifold $M$, there is an intrinsic dynamics derived from the so-called {\it Reeb vector field }${\mathcal R}$, which is characterized by the conditions \begin{equation}i_{\mathcal R}\omega=0 and i_{\mathcal R}\eta=1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, if $H:M\to {\mathbb R}$ is a Hamiltonian function one can define the Hamiltonian vector field of $H$ characterized by \begin{equation}i_{X_H^{(\omega,\eta)}}\omega=dH-{\mathcal R}(H)\eta and i_{X_H^{(\omega,\eta)}}\eta=0,\end{equation} (see for instance, \cite{CaLeLa ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: It is remarkable that, given a Hamiltonian $H$, one can modify $(\omega, \eta)$ and obtain a new cosymplectic structure $(\omega_H=\omega+ dH\wedge \eta,\eta)$ such that the evolution vector field $E^{(\omega,\eta) _H=X_H^{(\omega ,\eta) +\mathcal{R}$ of $H$ with respect to $(\omega,\eta)$ is just the Reeb vector field ${\mathcal R _H$ of $(\omega_H,\eta)$ (see Proposition \ref{cc ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: For time-dependent Hamiltonian systems, and once a reference frame has been fixed, the phase space is just the product ${\Bbb R}\times T^*Q.$ The canonical coordinate $t$ in ${\Bbb R}$ represents the time and $T^*Q$ is the phase space of positions and momenta.
elements.sent-2411-024: In addition, for a time-dependent Hamiltonian function $H:{\Bbb R}\times T^*Q\to {\Bbb R}$, the integral curves of the evolution vector field $E^{(\omega,\eta) _H$ are just the solutions of the Hamiltonian equations for $H.$ Following Marsden-Weinstein reduction, in \cite{A C.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Albert proposed a reduction theorem for cosymplectic structures, which has been extended in the singular setting in \cite{LS .}
elements.sent-2411-026: For this purpose, Albert introduces the notion of a Hamiltonian cosymplectic action as follows.
elements.sent-2411-027: An action of a Lie group $G$ on a cosymplectic manifold $(M,\omega,\eta)$ preserving $\omega$ and $\eta$ is Hamiltonian if there is a $G$-equivariant map $J: M\to \mathfrak{g ^*$, called the momentum map, such that \begin{equation}i_{\xi_M}\omega=dJ_\xi,\qquad for all \xi \in {\mathfrak g}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this setting, there is also a Noether theorem, since ${\mathcal R (J_\xi)=0$, that is, the functions $J_\xi$ are first integrals of the Reeb vector field (this method has been recently used in \cite{LuMaZa ).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, Albert's reduction Theorem has several limitations.
elements.sent-2411-030: For instance, in order to induce a cosymplectic structure $(\omega _\mu ,\eta _\mu )$ on the reduced manifold $M_\mu = J^{-1 (\mu )/G_\mu,$ one needs to impose the condition \begin{equation}\label{intro:cA} \eta(\xi_M)=0,\qquad for all \xi\in {\mathfrak g}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, there exist Hamiltonian cosymplectic actions for interesting invariant time-dependent Hamiltonian systems which do not satisfy condition (\ref{intro:cA ).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Even more impactful is the limitation posed by Noether's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-033: In fact, for a time-dependent Hamiltonian system on $M={\Bbb R}\times T^*Q$ with Reeb vector field ${\mathcal R =\displaystyle\frac{\partial {\partial t ,$ since ${\mathcal R (J_\xi)=0, $ then conserved quantities do not depend explicitly on time.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: On its own, this fact is already unsatisfactory for a theory whose main interest is to deal with time-dependent Hamiltonian systems.
elements.sent-2411-035: Moreover, it goes against the spirit of any relativity theory (for instance, Galilean relativity).
elements.sent-2411-036: It would be desirable to have a theory that, assuming it is applicable to some physical system, it continues to apply to the same system but described by a different inertial observer.
elements.sent-2411-037: The simplest example would be the position of the center of mass of a $N$ particle system subject to no external force: when described by an observer moving with the system, it would be constant.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, for any other inertial observer it would depend explicitly on time (linearly on this example).
elements.sent-2411-039: This line of thought provides a virtually infinite family of examples for our theory by considering time-independent Hamiltonian systems described by different inertial observers (one such example is considered in Example \ref{example1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-040: It could be argued that Albert's reduction Theorem could be applied to this systems if the ``right'' observer is chosen, but as discussed before this would be against the relativity principle.
elements.sent-2411-041: Moreover, there are examples which are intrinsically different from the previous ones in which Albert's reduction Theorem cannot be applied in any case, and therefore, a new reduction process should be proposed (see Section \ref{sec:planewave for one such example).}
elements.sent-2411-042: In another direction, time-dependent Hamiltonian systems may be discussed in the so-called extended formalism.
elements.sent-2411-043: In this approach, the extended phase space is a symplectic manifold: the total space of a principal $\R$-bundle on the phase space of momenta associated with the time-dependent Hamiltonian system.
elements.sent-2411-044: In addition, each symmetric time-dependent Hamiltonian system induces a symmetric Hamiltonian system on the symplectic extended phase space.
elements.sent-2411-045: So, one may apply standard Marsden-Weinstein symplectic reduction to this last system and, as a consequence, you can obtain the corresponding results for the time-dependent Hamiltonian system.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Aim and contributions of the paper
elements.sent-2411-047: In this paper, we try to overcome the limitations of Albert's reduction method in the application of the reduction of symmetric time-dependent Hamiltonian systems.
elements.sent-2411-048: First of all, we propose a modification of the action and the moment map in order to satisfy (\ref{intro:cA ).}
elements.sent-2411-049: Since this approach is not completely satisfactory (from a dynamic perspective) because we need to know the flow of the Reeb vector field, we weaken the notion of a cosymplectic structure.
elements.sent-2411-050: More precisely, we introduce the notion of a mechanical presymplectic structure on an odd-dimensional manifold $M$ as a closed 2-form $\omega$ of corank $1$ and a vector field ${\mathcal R}$ which generates the characteristic foliation of $\omega$, \begin{equation}\ker\omega=\langle {\mathcal R}\rangle.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-051: Any cosymplectic structure induces a mechanical presymplectic structure but there are examples of mechanical presymplectic structures which do not come from any cosymplectic ones (see Section \ref{section4 ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: If we have a Lie group acting on a manifold endowed with a mechanical presymplectic structure such that.
elements.sent-2411-054: There exists a smooth map $J:M\to {\mathfrak g ^*$ such that \begin{equation}i_{\xi_M}\omega=dJ_\xi, \forall \xi\in {\mathfrak g}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-055: This result is then used to obtain a correct reduction of the evolution dynamics of a time-dependent Hamiltonian system on a cosymplectic manifold.
elements.sent-2411-056: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-057: In Section \ref{section2 , we introduce the notion of a cosymplectic structure, we recall its basic properties and we describe two geometric formulations of time-dependent Hamiltonian dynamics using cosymplectic structures.}
elements.sent-2411-058: In Section \ref{sec:3 , we present Albert's reduction procedure for cosymplectic structures and describe the problems that arise.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Moreover, we present a modification of the action in order to solve these issues and the drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-060: In Section \ref{section4 , we introduce the notion of a mechanical presymplectic structure and a natural reduction procedure for this kind of structures.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Combining the results of the two previous sections, we obtain a method to reduce the evolution dynamics of a cosymplectic Hamiltonian system (see Section \ref{sec:5 ).}
elements.sent-2411-062: In Section \ref{section:examples , we present several examples in order to illustrate our theory.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The paper closes with an appendix which includes the proof of an algebraic result (Lemma \ref{Lema:4.5 ).}
elements.section-2411-002: Cosymplectic Hamiltonian systems and time-dependent Hamiltonian dynamics
elements.sent-2411-064: A {\it cosymplectic structure on a $(2n+1)$-dimensional manifold $M$ is a closed $2$-form $\omega\in\Omega^2(M)$ and a closed $1$-form $ \eta\in \Omega^1(M)$ such that $\eta\wedge \omega^n$ is a volume form on $M.$ In such a case, we say that $(M,\omega,\eta)$ is a {\it cosymplectic manifold.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Any cosymplectic manifold has an intrinsic dynamics derived from {\it the Reeb vector field }${\mathcal R}$, a vector field on $M$ characterized by the following conditions \begin{equation}\label{reeb} i_{\mathcal R}\omega=0 and i_{\mathcal R}\eta=1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-066: Note that these conditions imply that the cosymplectic structure $(\omega,\eta)$ is invariant with respect to the Reeb vector field, {\it i.e. \begin{equation}\label{ROE} {\mathcal L}_{\mathcal R}\omega=0 and {\mathcal L}_{\mathcal R}\eta=0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Moreover, \begin{equation}\ker\omega=\langle {\mathcal R}\rangle\end{equation} and we have the following decomposition of the tangent bundle \begin{equation}TM=\langle \eta\rangle ^0 \oplus \langle {\mathcal R}\rangle\end{equation} given by \begin{equation}X=(X-\eta(X){\mathcal R})+ \eta(X){\mathcal R},\end{equation} for all $X\in TM$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Here $\langle \eta\rangle ^0$ is the annihilator of $\eta$, i.e. \begin{equation}\langle \eta\rangle ^0=\{X\in TM/\eta(X)=0\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-069: A cosymplectic manifold is a Poisson manifold, where the Poisson bivector is given by \begin{equation}\Lambda(\alpha_1,\alpha_2)=\omega(\flat^{-1}(\alpha_1),\flat^{-1}(\alpha_2)),\end{equation} with $\alpha_1,\alpha_2 \in \Omega^1(M)$ and $\flat:{\mathfrak X (M)\to \Omega^1(M)$ the following isomorphism of $C^\infty(M,{\mathbb R )$-modules \begin{equation}\label{eq:bemol} \flat(X)=i_X\omega + \eta(X)\eta,\;\;\; X\in {\mathfrak X}(M).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Cosymplectic manifolds are the odd dimensional analogues of symplectic manifolds in the Poisson framework.
elements.sent-2411-071: As in the case of symplectic manifolds, there is a Darboux Theorem for these kind of manifolds, which states that there always exist local coordinates $(q^i, p_i,t)$ on $M$ such that the local expression of the cosymplectic structure and the associated Reeb vector field are \begin{equation}\label{eq:local} \omega=dq^i\wedge dp_i,\;\;\,\; \eta=dt,\;\;\;\; {\mathcal R}=\frac{\partial }{\partial t}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-072: Now, additionally, we suppose that we have a Hamiltonian function $H:M\to {\mathbb R .$ Denote by $X_H^{(\omega,\eta)}\in {\mathfrak X (M)$ the {\it Hamiltonian vector field of $H$ characterized by the following conditions \begin{equation}\label{XH} i_{X_H^{(\omega,\eta)}}\omega=dH-{\mathcal R}(H)\eta and i_{X_H^{(\omega,\eta)}}\eta=0\end{equation} and by $E^{(\omega,\eta) _H$ the {\it evolution vector field defined as the sum of $X^{(\omega,\eta) _H$ and the Reeb vector field ${\mathcal R}$, i.e. \begin{equation}\label{eq:EH} E^{(\omega,\eta)}_H := X^{(\omega,\eta)}_H + \mathcal{R}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Introducing \eqref{eq:local and \eqref{XH into \eqref{eq:EH , we obtain the local expressions of the Hamiltonian vector field of $H$ and of the evolution vector field, which are \begin{equation}\label{XHEH} X_H^{(\omega,\eta)}=\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}\frac{\partial }{\partial q^i}-\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}\frac{\partial }{\partial p_i},\;\;\;E^{(\omega,\eta)}_H=\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}\frac{\partial }{\partial q^i}-\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}\frac{\partial }{\partial p_i}+ \frac{\partial }{\partial t}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}\label{XHEH} X_H^{(\omega,\eta)}=\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}\frac{\partial }{\partial q^i}-\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}\frac{\partial }{\partial p_i},\;\;\;E^{(\omega,\eta)}_H=\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}\frac{\partial }{\partial q^i}-\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}\frac{\partial }{\partial p_i}+ \frac{\partial }{\partial t}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-074: Unlike for the Reeb vector field, the cosymplectic structure $(\omega,\eta)$ is not, in general, invariant with respect to the Hamiltonian and the evolution vector fields.
elements.sent-2411-075: In fact, we have \begin{equation}{\mathcal L}_{X_H^{(\omega,\eta)}}\omega={\mathcal L}_{E^{(\omega,\eta)}_H}\omega=-d({\mathcal R}(H))\wedge \eta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-076: For more information on cosymplectic geometry and the previous dynamical vector fields see, for instance, \cite{CNY and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The geometric structure of the cosymplectic manifold captures how the dynamics of the system evolves over time.
elements.sent-2411-078: It is important to remark that there are several geometric formulations of time-dependent Hamiltonian systems using cosymplectic structures.
elements.sent-2411-079: We will recall two of them in what follows.

elements.sent-2411-193: Summarizing, Albert's reduction Theorem has several limitations:.

elements.sent-2411-199: But, for our purpose, it would be enough that they were only first integrals of the evolution vector field of the Hamiltonian function.
elements.sent-2411-200: In this section, we will see how presymplectic structures of corank 1 with parallelizable characteristic foliation provide an appropriate setting for overcoming the previous limitations.
elements.sent-2411-201: Let $M$ be a manifold of dimension $(2n+1)$.
elements.sent-2411-202: A mechanical presymplectic structure on $M$ is a couple $(\omega, {\mathcal R )$, where $\omega$ is a closed 2-form of corank 1 and ${\mathcal R}$ is a vector field which generates the characteristic foliation of $\omega$, that is, \begin{equation}\ker\omega=\langle {\mathcal R}\rangle.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-203: The triple $(M,\omega, \mathcal{R )$ is called a mechanical presymplectic manifold.}
elements.sent-2411-204: It is clear that cosymplectic structures on a manifold $M$ of odd dimension are mechanical presymplectic structures on $M$.
elements.sent-2411-205: However, there exist mechanical presymplectic structures on manifolds of odd dimension which do not admit cosymplectic structures as we will see below.

elements.sent-2411-336: An interesting example is provided by a free electron, so $V_0(\mathbf q) = 0$, which is perturbed by a linearly polarized monocromatic plane wave (for instance a laser), i.e. \begin{equation}\varphi = 0, \qquad\qquad \mathbf A = A_0 \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \cos (\mathbf k \cdot \mathbf q - \omega t) \\\end{equation} where $\mathbf k$ is the wave--vector (recall that $\omega = | \mathbf k | c$) and $\boldsymbol \epsilon$ is the polarization vector (with $\boldsymbol \epsilon^2=1$).
